programs.name: KanCare Frail Elderly Waiver (FE Waiver)

eligibility.income_limits: No set income limit for the waiver, but beneficiaries keep $2,982 per month of their income (2026 amount). Income eligibility determined through underlying KanCare Medicaid categories such as SSI, 300% SSI, TANF, or Medically Needy spend-down.[1][2][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Standard Kansas Medicaid asset limit applies (exact dollar amount not specified in sources; use KanCare Spend Down Calculator for estimate). Countable assets exclude primary home (if equity ≤ $752,000 in 2026, applicant lives there or intends to return, spouse/minor/disabled child resides there), household furnishings, appliances, personal effects, and one vehicle. 60-month Look-Back Rule penalizes asset transfers below fair market value.[1]
other_requirements: Kansas resident
Eligible for KanCare (Kansas Medicaid)

Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) determined via Functional Assessment Instrument (FAI), assessing Activities of Daily Living (ADLs: toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, eating) and Instrumental ADLs (IADLs: cleaning, cooking, shopping, paying bills), plus cognitive/behavioral issues.[1][2][3]

benefits.value: Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) as alternative to nursing home care, including variety of services authorized via Plan of Care (POC) process to meet needs of frail elderly; specific services not exhaustively listed but designed for integration and maintenance in community. Does not cover room/board or living expenses.[4][5]

methods: Contact Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services (KDADS) or local waiver program manager for preliminary functional eligibility application (specific phone/website not in results; check KDADS or KanCare sites)
Enroll via fiscal agent for providers, but for members: apply for KanCare Medicaid first, then waiver assessment[4][5]
application.waitlist: Waitlist (proposed recipient list) maintained when no openings exist; limited slots[3]
forms: Preliminary functional eligibility application with waiver program manager[3]
Specific waiver eligibility assessment and re-assessments[4]
documents: Proof of KanCare Medicaid eligibility
Documentation for NFLOC assessment (e.g., medical records, FAI results)
Financial documents for Medicaid eligibility (income, assets)
Requests for LOC documentation may include waiver eligibility assessments[4]

geography.restrictions: None specified; serves Kansas residents statewide[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Wait times may vary regionally due to limited slots and local providers, but no specific differences detailed[3]
offices_or_providers: Managed through KDADS; providers enroll via fiscal agent (KMAP); MCOs like Sunflower Health Plan involved[4][5]

gotchas: Must first qualify for KanCare Medicaid; waiver denial if not financially eligible[2]
60-month Look-Back Rule on asset transfers leads to penalty periods[1]
Limited slots create waitlists; not guaranteed immediate access[3]
Services require prior authorization via POC; no coverage for room/board[4][5]
Home equity limit $752,000 (2026); intent to return required for exemption[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: No fixed income limit but patient liability of $2,982/month (2026); NFLOC via FAI assessment; statewide with waitlist due to slot limits; ties to underlying Medicaid eligibility

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for PACE eligibility itself[3]. However, if seeking Medicaid-funded PACE (which covers ~99% of participants[5]), Medicaid income limits apply: under 300% of Federal Benefit Rate (~$2,901/month for single individuals in 2025)[2]. Kansas-specific Medicaid income thresholds should be verified with state.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits for PACE eligibility itself[3]. For Medicaid-funded PACE: assets must be $2,000 or less, excluding primary home and one automobile[2][5].
other_requirements: Must be certified by the state as needing nursing home level of care[3][7]
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services at time of enrollment[3][7]
Must reside in the service area of a PACE organization[3][7]
Persons age 55-64 must be determined disabled by Social Security standards[1]
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C), Medicare prepayment plan, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice services[3]
For Medicare eligibility: must be U.S. citizen or legal resident for 5 years; must be 65+, disabled, have ALS, or end-stage renal disease[2]

benefits.value: Comprehensive package including: all Medicaid-covered services, multidisciplinary assessment and treatment planning, primary care (physician and nursing), social work services, restorative therapies (physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology), personal care and supportive services, nutritional counseling, recreational therapy, transportation, meals, and medical specialty services[4]
benefits.varies_by: not_applicable — all participants receive the same comprehensive benefit package regardless of payment source[4]

methods: Referral through Kansas Department of Aging and Disability Services (KDADS) using form ES-3166[1]
Contact PACE provider directly (see providers below)
Coordinate with eligibility worker who will communicate with PACE entity[1]
forms: ES-3166 (official notification form for PACE referrals and case changes)[1]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources — contact PACE provider for required documentation

geography.restrictions: PACE is only available in service areas of approved Kansas providers[1]
geography.regional_variations: Three approved PACE providers serve different regions of Kansas[1][4]
offices_or_providers: Via Christi HOPE (Wichita) — serves Sedgwick County[1][4]
Midland Care (Topeka) — serves Wyandotte, Leavenworth, Shawnee, Douglas, Jackson, Jefferson, Osage, Pottawatomie, and other counties[1][4][8]
Bluestem PACE (McPherson) — serves Marion, McPherson, Ottawa, Reno, Rice, and Saline counties[1][4]

gotchas: PACE is NOT available statewide — you must live in one of three specific service areas to qualify[1][4]

No income or asset limits for PACE itself, but ~99% of participants are dual-eligible (Medicare + Medicaid) because Medicaid covers most costs[5]. Private pay is possible but costs $7,000+/month or $200-900/month share of cost[5]
You cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plans, or prescription drug plans to join PACE[3] — this is a common barrier for seniors already in MA plans
Persons age 55-64 face an additional hurdle: must be determined disabled by Social Security standards, not just need nursing home level of care[1]
Must be able to live safely in community at enrollment — PACE is not for those requiring immediate institutional care[3][7]

Medicaid eligibility pathways are complex and state-specific; not meeting standard income/asset limits doesn't disqualify you — Medicaid Planning Professionals can help explore alternative pathways[2]
Average PACE participant is 76 years old with multiple complex conditions[3] — program is designed for frail elderly with significant needs
programs.data_shape_notes: PACE in Kansas is geographically restricted to three provider service areas, making location the primary eligibility barrier. No income/asset test for PACE itself, but funding source (Medicare/Medicaid vs. private pay) dramatically affects cost. Dual-eligible status is nearly universal among participants. Age 55-64 cohort faces additional disability verification requirement. All participants receive identical comprehensive benefit package regardless of payment source.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Start here:** Determine if your loved one lives in one of the three PACE service areas[1][4]. If not, PACE is unavailable regardless of other qualifications.

**The income/asset confusion:** PACE itself has no income or asset limits[3]. However, if you want PACE covered by Medicaid (which funds ~99% of participants[5]), Medicaid's limits apply: roughly $2,901/month income and $2,000 in assets (excluding home and car)[2][5]. Private pay is an option but expensive[5].

**Age 55-64 barrier:** If your loved one is under 65, Social Security must determine them disabled — needing nursing home level of care alone isn't enough[1].

**Medicare Advantage trap:** If your loved one is in a Medicare Advantage plan, they must disenroll before joining PACE[3].

**Next step:** Contact your regional PACE provider directly or work with your eligibility worker to submit form ES-3166[1]. Processing timelines and specific documentation requirements should be confirmed directly with the provider, as these details are not publicly specified in Kansas guidance.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) including QMB, SLMB, QI

eligibility.income_limits: Income limits vary by program tier and are based on monthly amounts including a $20 disregard per household. For 2026 (most recent available): QMB - Individual: $1,275; Couple: $1,724. SLMB - Individual: approx. $1,549 (based on 120% FPL trends from 2022 data of $1,379 and 2024 adjustments); Couple: approx. $2,080. QI - Individual: approx. $1,660; Couple: approx. $2,239 (up to 135% FPL). Older Kansas-specific data (2024): QMB Individual $1,255 / Couple $1,693; LMB (SLMB) Individual $1,149 / Couple $1,551. Limits updated annually; confirm with KDHE as they align with federal FPL but state-administered[1][2][3][5][7][8]. No full household size table beyond individual/couple in sources; assumes 1-2 person households typical for Medicare beneficiaries.
eligibility.asset_limits: Resource limits (assets): Individual: $9,430 (2024/2026 QMB); $8,400 (older 2022 data). Married couple: $14,130 (2024/2026); $12,600 (2022). What counts: Bank accounts, stocks, bonds (countable resources). Exempt: Primary home, one car, household goods, life insurance, burial plots, prepaid funeral (up to certain amounts), $1,500 burial allowance. Limits updated periodically; QI/SLMB similar to QMB[1][2][4][5].
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A (for QMB, SLMB, QI; QDWI exception for working disabled).
Kansas resident.
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant.
Not eligible for full Medicaid (MSP is for higher income).
QI requires annual reapplication and is first-come, first-served with priority to prior recipients[2][3][4][6].

benefits.value: QMB: Pays Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, copayments for Medicare-covered services/items; auto-qualifies for Extra Help (Part D low-income subsidy, caps drug copays at $12.65/drug in 2026). SLMB: Pays Part B premiums only (plus Part D via Extra Help). QI: Pays Part B premiums only (plus Part D Extra Help); no deductibles/coinsurance. Providers cannot bill QMB enrollees for Medicare-covered costs. Value: Saves ~$2,000+/year in premiums/cost-sharing[1][2][3][4][5][6][8].

methods: In-person or phone: Local Kansas Department for Children and Families (DCF) Service Center (find via kdhe.ks.gov or call 1-800-792-4884)[3][8].
Phone: 1-800-792-4884 (Kansas Legal Services confirmation line for guidelines)[8].
Mail: Submit 4-page MSP application to local DCF office.
No specific online URL listed; apply through DCF centers (khap.kdhe.ks.gov for policy/docs)[3][6].
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; typically state-processed, begins month of application for determined eligibility[2][6].
application.waitlist: QI has first-come, first-served with waitlist possible if funding exhausted; priority to prior-year recipients[2].
forms: 4-page Medicare Savings Program application (KC-2700 or similar brochure-attached form)[3][8].
documents: Proof of income (Social Security award letters, pay stubs).
Proof of resources/assets (bank statements).
Medicare card.
Proof of residency, identity (ID, utility bill).
Household size details.

geography.restrictions: Available statewide in Kansas via DCF; no county restrictions noted.
geography.regional_variations: Administered locally via DCF Service Centers; potential variation in processing based on local office workload, but no specific differences detailed[3][4].
offices_or_providers: Local DCF Service Centers (locate via kdhe.ks.gov); Area Agencies on Aging like NEKAAA for assistance[3][4].

programs.official_source: https://khap.kdhe.ks.gov (Kansas Department of Health and Environment/Kansas Health Assistance Programs); local DCF centers
gotchas: Income/resource limits change annually (Feb/May FPL updates); always confirm current via 1-800-792-4884[5][6][8].
QI requires annual reapplication and may have funding caps/waitlists[2].
QMB protects from provider billing for Medicare-covered services, but small Medicaid copays possible[2].
Auto-qualifies for Extra Help, but must report changes promptly.
Not full Medicaid; doesn't cover long-term care.
Outdated web data (e.g., 2022 limits); use state sources for 2026[1][4].
Married couple limits apply if both on Medicare; household size mainly 1-2.
programs.data_shape_notes: State-administered with federal FPL-based tiers (QMB 100%, SLMB 120%, QI 135%); income/assets for individual/couple only (no larger household table); annual updates and QI caps create variability; local DCF application required, no central online portal.

programs.name: Food Assistance (SNAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Households with a member 60+ or disabled are exempt from the gross income limit (130% FPL) and only need to meet the net income limit. For example, 2025 limits: $15,060 gross/month for 1 person, $20,440 for 2 (general reference; Kansas uses expanded rules for elderly). Net income is gross minus deductions like standard $209, medical over $35 for elderly/disabled, shelter (rent/utilities exceeding 50% income after other deductions, capped at $744 unless elderly/disabled). Social Security, pensions, VA/disability count as income.[1][2][3][4][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable assets below $2,750, or $4,250 if a household member is elderly (60+) or disabled. Countable: cash, bank accounts, non-exempt property. Exempt: primary home, household goods, retirement savings, life insurance cash value (varies), income-producing property (details via DCF).[1][2]
other_requirements: Kansas resident; apply in county of residence.
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen with legal status documentation.
Household includes those who buy/prepare food together.

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card for food purchases (groceries, not hot food/alcohol/tobacco). Maximum allotments (Oct 2025-Sept 2026, 48 states): 1-person $298; 2 $546; 3 $785; 4 $994; 5 $1,183; 6 $1,421; 7 $1,571; 8 $1,789 (+$218 each additional). Actual amount: max allotment minus 30% of net income.[4]

methods: Online: Kansas Benefits Portal at https://www.benefitsparkansas.org/ (implied via DCF sites).
Phone: Contact local DCF office (find via https://www.dcf.ks.gov/services/ees/Pages/default.aspx).
Mail/In-person: Local DCF Economic and Employment Services office by county.
Authorized rep/home visit/telephone interview for elderly/disabled.
application.processing_time: Typically 30 days; expedited for urgent cases (7 days if very low income/liquid assets under $100).[3]
forms: Food Assistance Application (via DCF portal or office; no specific form number listed)
documents: Proof of ID, residency, income (pay stubs, Social Security award letters, pensions), expenses (medical bills over $35/month for elderly, rent/utilities, shelter costs), citizenship/immigration status, assets.

geography.regional_variations: Administered by county DCF offices; potential processing/wait differences by county, but no specific variations detailed.
offices_or_providers: Local Kansas DCF Economic and Employment Services offices by county.

gotchas: Elderly households skip gross income test but must meet net income and assets; deduct high medical/shelter costs.
No broad-based categorical eligibility in Kansas (stricter than some states).
Assets include countable resources; exemptions often missed (e.g., home exempt).
Social Security/pensions count as income.
Apply in correct county; authorized reps allowed for mobility issues.
programs.data_shape_notes: Elderly/disabled exempt from gross income test; higher asset limit; deductions for medical/shelter uncapped for them; county-based application; benefits scale by household size and net income.

programs.name: Low-Income Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Combined gross monthly income (before deductions) of all persons living at the address must not exceed 150% of the federal poverty level. 2026 guidelines: 1: $1,956; 2: $2,644; 3: $3,331; 4: $4,019; 5: $4,706; 6: $5,394; 7: $6,081; 8: $6,769; +1: add $688 per additional person.[4][2]
eligibility.asset_limits: Available resources are considered in eligibility, but no specific dollar limits or exemptions detailed in sources.[4]
other_requirements: An adult living at the address must be personally responsible for paying heating costs, payable to landlord or fuel vendor.[2][4][5]
Household must live in a Kansas county and be legally capable of acting on own behalf (or via guardian/conservator if incapacitated).[1]
At least one U.S. citizen or qualified alien in household; proof required if not on other assistance.[1][5]
Everyone residing at the same utility address must apply together; college students in dorms ineligible, must live at home full-time to count.[1]
No duplicate benefits from state LIEAP and tribal LIHEAP in same year; tribal members referred to tribe.[1]
No more than one regular benefit per program year.[1]

benefits.value: One-time payment to help meet home energy costs (heating fuel); exact amount varies by household income, size, dwelling type, heating fuel type, and utility rates. Subject to available federal funding.[4][6]

methods: Online: DCF Self-Service Portal at www.dcf.ks.gov (click 'Apply for Services').[2][3]
Paper: Request from local DCF Service Center, helping agencies, utilities, or prior recipients mailed info.[2][5]
In-person: Local DCF Service Centers, Evergy Wichita Connect walk-in center, or Kansas LIEAP application events (list on DCF website).[3]
Submit to designated DCF Regional LIEAP processing office.[5]
forms: Separate LIEAP application (specific to program, includes heating/cooling account numbers, fuel type); requires signatures of all adults in residence.[2][5]
documents: Proof of income (earned/unearned) for all in residence (e.g., VA/SSA/SSI/pension award letters, recent tax return for self-employment).[2][6]
Copies of fuel bills (gas, electric, propane, etc.).[2]
Proof of child support payments or court order.[2]
Rental/lease agreement if heating in rent, subsidized housing, or bills in landlord's name.[2]
Other info as requested.[2]

geography.restrictions: Living in a Kansas county; tribal members referred to tribe (United Tribes of Kansas/Southeast Nebraska, Kickapoo Tribe).[1]
geography.regional_variations: Submitted to designated DCF Regional LIEAP processing offices; possible differences in events/wait times, but no specific variations detailed.[3][5]
offices_or_providers: Local DCF Service Centers, regional processing offices.[2][5]

gotchas: Everyone at the address (not just applicant) income counted, unlike TANF/FA which may exclude others.[5]
Must be personally responsible for heating costs; ineligible if fully included in subsidized rent.[2][5]
College students don't count unless living full-time at home (no dorms/apartments).[1]
Separate LIEAP app required, not auto from other benefits; application period only (e.g., 2026: Jan 20-Mar 31).[4][5]
No duplicates with tribal programs; one benefit per year.[1]
Legally incapacitated ineligible without guardian.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income based on full household at address (not just applicant family); varies by income/size/fuel/dwelling/utility rates; seasonal app period ends late March; regional DCF processing.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: Household income must either include a member who received TAF, SSI, or LIEAP within the last 12 months, or not exceed 200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines (specific dollar amounts not listed in sources; refer to current KHRC State Plan). Exact table not provided in results, but typical levels are 200% FPL by household size[1][2][6].
other_requirements: Household must occupy the home; all utilities (electric and gas) must be active[1][2].
Home not designated for acquisition/clearance within 12 months[1][2].
If previously weatherized, must be >15 years ago (unless disaster damage not covered by insurance); no repeat of prior measures[1][2].
Homeowners or renters (renters need landlord permission)[1][3][6].
No major health/safety issues (e.g., mold, asbestos, threat of violence) or structural problems that make weatherization ineffective or unsafe[2].

benefits.value: Free weatherization services including certified energy audits and professional installation of upgrades (e.g., insulation, sealing, health/safety improvements); no specific dollar amounts or hours stated, all at no cost[1][3][4][6][8].

methods: Online: https://kshousingcorp.org/weatherization-assistance[1].
Standardized application form: https://kshousingcorp.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Standardized-Weatherization-Application.pdf[1].

Regional providers: e.g., NCRPC (800) 432-0303 or download/mail/email form[3]; SEK-CAP (contact via https://sek-cap.com/services/weatherization/)[4]; SCKEDD (apply now link on site)[8]; Evergy area agencies[6].
Phone/mail/in-person via local subgrantees (contact KHRC or local agency).
forms: Notarized Affidavit of No Income if applicable[1].
documents: Income verification for all members (past 3 months: paystubs, SSI/TAF/LIEAP docs, unemployment, public assistance)[1][3].
Household info: names, birth dates, gender[1].
Utility company names and account numbers[1].
Landlord info/permission if renter[1][6].
Notarized Affidavit of No Income if applicable[1].

geography.restrictions: Administered statewide by Kansas Housing Resources Corporation (KHRC) with local subgrantees/providers by region (e.g., NCRPC, SEK-CAP, SCKEDD for specific counties)[1][3][4][8].
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered via regional community action agencies or providers; apply through local agency serving your area; potential differences in wait times or processes not detailed[3][4][6][8].
offices_or_providers: KHRC primary: https://kshousingcorp.org/weatherization-assistance[1].
NCRPC[3].
SEK-CAP (Southeast Kansas)[4].
SCKEDD (South Central KS counties: Butler, Chautauqua, etc.)[8].
Evergy area agencies[6].

gotchas: No services if home weatherized in past 15 years (unless disaster exception); no repeats of prior measures[1][2].
Renters need landlord permission and agreement not to raise rent for 2 years[6].
Active utilities required; home must be occupiable without major safety/structural issues[1][2].
Eligibility prioritizes SSI/TAF/LIEAP recipients; income verification strictly required for past 12 months[1][2].
Apply via local provider, not centrally; find agency by region[3][4][6][8].
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide via KHRC with regional subgrantees; priority for SSI/TAF/LIEAP households; 15-year repeat restriction; income at 200% FPL or categorical eligibility; free services only after audit.

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Counseling for Kansas (SHICK)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; available to anyone with Medicare questions, including older Kansans, people with disabilities, and caregivers[1][2][4][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[2][4]
other_requirements: Open to all Kansans with questions about Medicare or related health insurance; no affiliation requirement beyond having Medicare-related inquiries[1][2][4][7]

benefits.value: Free, confidential, one-on-one counseling on Medicare (Parts A-D), Medicare Supplement (Medigap), Medicare Advantage, prescription drug coverage (Part D), Extra Help, long-term care insurance, claims/appeals, supplemental rate comparisons, employer group plans, Medicaid, and other health insurance options; public education presentations; available Monday-Friday, typically 8:30 a.m.-3 p.m. or 8 a.m.-5 p.m. via phone or in-person[1][2][3][4][5][7][8]

methods: Phone: 1-800-860-5260 (statewide toll-free)[2][3][8][9]
Regional/local contacts (e.g., Sedgwick: 316-660-0126[1], Johnson County: 913-715-8856[5], North Central: 1-800-432-2703[6])
Website: https://www.kdads.ks.gov/services-programs/aging/medicare-programs/senior-health-insurance-counseling-for-kansas-shick[2][3]
In-person: Through local Area Agencies on Aging or SHICK counselors statewide[1][5][6][8]
Mail/Fax: Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services, 503 S. Kansas Ave, Topeka, KS 66603; Fax: 785-296-0256[3][8]
application.processing_time: Counseling appointments scheduled upon contact; no formal processing as it's direct service, though busy during Open Enrollment (mid-October to early December)[6]
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned; appointments available via phone[1][2][4][5]
forms: No specific forms required; schedule appointment directly[2][4]
documents: Medicare card, current insurance documents, prescription lists recommended for counseling sessions but not mandatory for scheduling[7]

geography.regional_variations: Delivered through local Area Agencies on Aging and volunteer counselors with regional contacts and offices (e.g., Central Plains AAA in Wichita: 800-367-7298 ext. 5132, Wyandotte/Leavenworth AAA in Kansas City: 888-661-1444); no differences in services, but local providers handle appointments[1][5][6][8]
offices_or_providers: Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services, 503 S. Kansas Ave, Topeka, KS 66603[3][8]
Local examples: Sedgwick County (Wichita)[1], Johnson County[5], North Central region[6]

gotchas: Not an insurance provider or sales program—counselors are independent volunteers not affiliated with insurers[1][2][4][8]
Busy during Medicare Open Enrollment (mid-October to early December), so call early[6]
Free service, not financial aid or direct healthcare—focuses on education and assistance[2][7]
Available to anyone with Medicare questions, not just seniors[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; counseling service via statewide volunteer network through Area Agencies on Aging; part of federal SHIP program

programs.name: Kansas Senior Nutrition Program (Home-Delivered and Congregate Meals)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits specified; open to all individuals 60+ and spouses. Local providers may have additional criteria—contact them directly.[2]
other_requirements: Spouses of individuals 60+ are eligible regardless of age.[2]
Program volunteers are eligible for congregate meals.[2]
For home-delivered: Typically requires homebound status or functional impairment, but contact local provider for exact assessment.[2]

benefits.value: Nutritious meals in group/congregate settings or home-delivered to residence; nutrition education on food choices, physical activity, and health conditions.[2]

methods: Phone: Local Aging and Disability Resource Center at 855-200-ADRC (2372)[2]
Phone: KDADS at 785-296-0373[2]
Email: KDADSOAASCA@ks.gov[2]
Mail: Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services, Attention: Long Term Services & Supports, 503 S. Kansas Ave., Topeka, KS 66603-3404[2]
In-person: Local Aging and Disability Resource Center or provider for registration and assessment[2]
application.processing_time: Not specified; varies by local provider.[2]
application.waitlist: Possible depending on local capacity; contact provider.[2]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; registration via local assessment.[2]
documents: Not explicitly listed; prepare proof of age (e.g., ID) and any income/need details if requested locally.[2]

geography.restrictions: None statewide; delivered through local providers.[2]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs) and providers; eligibility details, availability, and wait times may vary by region—contact local ADRC.[2]
offices_or_providers: Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (call 855-200-ADRC)[2]

gotchas: No statewide income test, but local providers determine full eligibility including homebound status for delivery—always confirm locally.[2]
Spouses qualify regardless of age, unlike some programs requiring 60+ for all.[2]
Funded under Older Americans Act; availability tied to federal/local funding and capacity.[2]
Distinct from Kansas Senior Farmers Market Nutrition Program, which has strict 185% FPL income limits and provides produce coupons only.[1][3]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income or asset limits statewide; priority on age 60+ and spouses; delivered via 15 local Area Agencies on Aging with potential regional capacity differences; requires local assessment for home-delivered.

programs.name: Caregiver Support Programs

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income or asset limits mentioned in sources; eligibility often tied to Medicaid waivers requiring nursing facility level of care. General KanCare guidelines exist but not detailed for caregivers.[4]
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified; no asset tests detailed for caregiver support programs.[1][2]
other_requirements: Caregivers of individuals 60+ or grandparents 60+ caring for grandchildren under 18.[2]
Family member or informal caregiver 18+ providing care, often for those meeting nursing facility level of care (e.g., frail elderly 65+, physical disabilities 16-64, I/DD 5+).[1]
For K-RAD: Care for individual with probable Alzheimer's/dementia, limited access to other supports.[5]

benefits.value: Respite care, personal care, homemaker services, support services; K-RAD subsidizes up to $1,000/year per care recipient for respite (paid to provider).[5][6] Specifics vary by waiver (e.g., HCBS for frail elderly).[1]

methods: Phone: Kansas Aging and Disability Resource Center at 1-855-200-2372 (ADRC).[7][8]
Email for K-RAD: donalds@eckaaa.org with two required forms (not named).[5]
Contact local Area Agency on Aging, e.g., Northeast Kansas (nekaaa.org), East Central (785-242-7200).[2][5]
application.processing_time: K-RAD: Processed in order received; funds first-come, first-served (fiscal year July-June).[5]
application.waitlist: Funding limited, no awards guaranteed; potential wait if funds exhausted.[5]
documents: Not explicitly listed; may include proof of caregiver status, care recipient diagnosis (e.g., Alzheimer's for K-RAD), background check for paid roles.[1][5]

geography.restrictions: Varies by county and Area Agency on Aging; targeted to frail elderly, disabilities.[1][6]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local Area Agencies on Aging (e.g., Northeast Kansas AAA, East Central AAA); services, providers, funding availability differ by region.[2][5][6]
offices_or_providers: Northeast Kansas Area Agency on Aging (nekaaa.org).[2]
East Central Area Agency on Aging (eckaaa.org, 785-242-7200).[5]
Kansas Aging and Disability Resource Center (statewide referrals).[8]

gotchas: Not a single program but network of waivers (e.g., Frail Elderly, I/DD) and local AAA services; requires nursing facility level of care determination.[1]
Funding limited (e.g., K-RAD $1,000 cap, first-come).[5]
Often requires enrollment in Medicaid/KanCare; spouses/family may qualify but need to live with care recipient and pass checks.[1]
Varies county-to-county; contact local AAA essential.[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by Area Agencies on Aging and waivers; no uniform income/asset tables; ties to Medicaid level of care; regional providers and limited funding pools.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income must not exceed 125% of the federal poverty level[2][6]. The search results do not provide specific dollar amounts or household-size tables. Families should contact their local provider to determine their exact income threshold based on household composition.
other_requirements: Must be unemployed[2][6]
Must have poor employment prospects[2][6]

Enrollment priority given to: veterans and qualified spouses, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, those with low literacy skills or limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless or at-risk individuals, and those with low employment prospects or prior unsuccessful American Job Center usage[2]

benefits.value: Paid work averaging 20 hours per week at the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage[2]. On-the-job training in computer or vocational skills, work experience for résumé, professional job placement assistance, and supportive services including dental, vision, clothing, and transportation assistance[3][7]

methods: Phone: Wichita Workforce Center at (316) 771-6750[5]
Phone: Jobs for Progress National (SER National)[5]
In-person: Wichita Workforce Center, 2021 N. Amidon, #1100, Wichita, KS 67203[5]
Online: AARP Foundation locator at https://my.aarpfoundation.org/locator/scsep/ to find local programs[8]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources. Contact local provider for requirements.

geography.restrictions: Program operates statewide through multiple providers[5][6]
geography.regional_variations: SER National operates SCSEP in California, Colorado, Kansas, Texas, and Wisconsin, serving over 3,000 participants in over 225 counties[6]. AARP Foundation also operates SCSEP in Kansas[7]. Specific regional variations in services or wait times are not detailed in available sources.
offices_or_providers: Wichita Workforce Center (primary contact)[5]
SER Jobs For Progress (operates program in Kansas)[3][6]
AARP Foundation SCSEP[7]
Additional providers through Kansas WorkforceONE system[3]

## Additional Context for Families

**Key Takeaway:** SCSEP is a **paid training program**, not a permanent job. Participants work part-time (averaging 20 hours/week) at nonprofits or public agencies while gaining skills and work experience, with the goal of transitioning to unsubsidized employment afterward[2][7].

**Income Verification is Critical:** The 125% federal poverty level threshold is the hard eligibility cap[2][6]. Since the search results do not provide 2026 dollar amounts, families should contact the Wichita Workforce Center at (316) 771-6750 immediately to confirm whether their loved one qualifies based on current household income[5].

**Multiple Entry Points:** Kansas residents can access SCSEP through SER Jobs For Progress, AARP Foundation, or other providers in the Kansas WorkforceONE system[3][5][6]. The AARP Foundation locator tool can help identify the nearest program[8].

**Supportive Services Matter:** Beyond wages and training, the program covers dental, vision, clothing, and transportation assistance[3]—valuable for low-income seniors facing barriers to employment.

**Priority Enrollment:** Veterans, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, and rural residents receive enrollment priority[2], which may affect wait times and placement speed.
